content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
SUPERSEDEDBY1998
AMENDMENTS TO
Practices Commission
REGULATION 18530
February 15,
1995
Rodney D.
Foster
Public Affairs Manager
City of Chino
13220 Centra Avenue
Chino,
CA
91708—0667
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-95—039
Dear Mr.
Foster:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of the City of Chino regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”) 1
Since your advice request is
general in nature,
we are treating your request as one for
informal assistance. 2
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
Further,
the Commission
cannot determine whether the expenditures for a television program
approved by the Chino City Council are legitimate government
expenditures.
(See, e.g.
Penal Code Section 424
Stansori v.
Mott
(1976)
17 Cal.3d 206.)
We can only advise as to whether such
expenditures are a violation of the provisions of the Political
Reform Act.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807
•
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807
•
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—95—039
Page
2
qUESTION
3
1.
Is a cable television program produced by the City of
Chino and hosted by the mayor a mass mailing?
2.
Is the cable television program produced by the City of
Chino and hosted by the mayor an expenditure of public funds for
the purpose of seeking elective office in violation of Section
85300 of the Act?
CONCLUSION
1.
A cable television program is not considered a mass
mailing under the Act.
2.
A cable television program produced by the City of Chino
and hosted by the mayor will not be considered an expenditure of
public funds for the purpose of seeking elective office in
violation of Section 85300 so long as the program is not used to
expressly advocate the election of any candidate.
FACTS
The City of Chino has a cable television program that
highlights city services.
The city wishes to produce a bi-weekly
30-minute program called “Chino Pride.”
The magazine-style
program would feature the manys positive aspects of the city and be
hosted by the mayor.
ANALYSIS
Mass Mailings
Section 89001 provides that “no newsletter or other mass
mailing shall be sent at public expense.”
Regulation 18901(a)
provides that a mass mailing is prohibited only if all the
following apply:
(1)
Any item sent is delivered,
by any
means, to the recipient at his or her
residence, place of employment or business,
or
post office box.
For purposes of subdivision
(a) (1), the item delivered to the recipient
must be a tangible item,
such as a videotape,
record,
or button,
or a written document.
Your third question was construed to be a public records act
request.
Since the timelines for public records act requests and
advice under the Political Reform Act are different,
we have
separated the third request and will respond to it independently.


Our File No.
1—95—039
Page
3
(2)
The item sent either:
(A)
Features an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which produces
or sends the mailing,
or
(B)
Includes the name, office,
photograph,
or other reference to an
elected officer affiliated with the
agency which produces or sends the
mailing,
and is prepared or sent in
cooperation, consultation, coordination,
or concert with the elected officer;
(3) (A)
Any of the costs of distribution
is paid for with public moneys;
or
(B)
Costs of design, production,
and printing exceeding $50.00 are paid
with public moneys,
and the design,
production,
or printing is done with the
intent of sending the item other than as
permitted by this regulation.
(4)
More than two hundred substantially
similar items are sent,
in a single calendar
month, excluding any4 item sent in response to
an unsolicited request and any item described
in subdivision
(b);
(Emphasis added.)
According to Regulation 18901,
a publicly-funded mailing is a
prohibited mass mailing if it is delivered as a tangible item to
the recipient at his or her residence, place of employment or
business,
or post office box.
Since distribution over the
airwaves is not the distribution of a tangible item, televised
programs are not prohibited mass mailings under the Act.
Expenditures for the Purpose of Seeking Elective Office
Section 85300 also sets forth a restriction on the use of
public funds.
Section 85300 provides:
No public officer shall expend and no
candidate shall accept any public moneys for the
purpose of seeking elective office.


Our File No.
1—95—039
Page
4
Thus,
Section 85300 prohibits the use of public moneys
4 for
the purpose of seeking elective office.
However, the Act would
not prohibit the use of public funds to pay for a television
program hosted by the mayor, provided the program is not used to
advocate the mayor’s
(or any other candidate’s)
election to
office.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-590l.
Sincerely
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
W.
Wallace
isel,
Legal Division
“Public moneys” is defined to include all bonds and evidence
of indebtedness,
and all moneys belonging to the state,
or any
city, county,
town, district,
or public agency therein,
and all
moneys, bonds,
and evidence of indebtedness received or held by
state,
county, district, city,
town,
or public agency officers in
their official capacity.
(Section 85102(e); Penal Code Section
426.)
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of
l0cz per page.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 3
1.
Is a cable television program produced by the City of
Chino and hosted by the mayor a mass mailing?
2.
Is the cable television program produced by the City of
Chino and hosted by the mayor an expenditure of public funds for
the purpose of seeking elective office in violation of Section
85300 of the Act?

CONCLUSION: 1.
A cable television program is not considered a mass
mailing under the Act.
2.
A cable television program produced by the City of Chino
and hosted by the mayor will not be considered an expenditure of
public funds for the purpose of seeking elective office in
violation of Section 85300 so long as the program is not used to
expressly advocate the election of any candidate.
